source_paper_title: Theoretical Smoothing Frameworks for Nonsmooth Simple Bilevel Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Alcantara_2026, title={Theoretical Smoothing Frameworks for Nonsmooth Simple Bilevel Problems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0405}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0405}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Alcantara, Jan Harold and Takeda, Akiko}, year={2026}, month=mar }
context:
Section 5 (Concluding Remarks and Future Directions), page 30 (PDF pagination as provided: page 30/32)

Another important direction is to extend our smoothing frameworks to settings where the lower-level constraint set is not fixed but instead depends on the upper-level variable x. A preliminary approach based on quadratic regularization combined with penalization has been proposed in [Liu et al., 2023]; however, it remains an open question whether the resulting approximation qualifies as a smoothing family in the sense of Definition 2.5 and whether it satisfies gradient consistency.
title: Smoothing value functions with upper-level-dependent lower-level feasible sets in bilevel optimization
background_latex:
Let \(X\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) be a closed convex set. For each \(x\in X\), let \(Y(x)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^m\) be a (possibly unbounded) closed feasible set that depends on \(x\). Let \(g:\mathbb{R}^n\times\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\) be a (possibly nonsmooth) Lipschitz continuous lower-level objective.

Define the lower-level value function
\[v(x):=\min_{y\in Y(x)} g(x,y),\]
and its solution set (argmin mapping)
\[S(x):=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{y\in Y(x)} g(x,y).\]

A family \(\{v_\mu:\mu>0\}\) is called a family of smooth approximations (a smoothing family) for \(v\) over an open set \(O\supseteq X\) if each \(v_\mu\) is continuously differentiable on \(O\) and
\[\lim_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} v_\mu(x)=v(\bar x)\qquad\text{for all }\bar x\in O.
\]

Let \(\partial v(\bar x)\) denote the Clarke generalized gradient of the locally Lipschitz function \(v\) at \(\bar x\). The smoothing family \(\{v_\mu\}\) satisfies gradient consistency at \(\bar x\) if
\[\emptyset\neq \limsup_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} \nabla v_\mu(x)\ \subseteq\ \partial v(\bar x),\]
where the outer limit \(\limsup\) is taken over all sequences \(x_k\to \bar x\), \(\mu_k\downarrow 0\) with \(\nabla v_{\mu_k}(x_k)\to \xi\).

The paper develops smoothing families \(v_\mu\) for the case of a fixed lower-level feasible set \(Y\) (independent of \(x\)), via quadratic or entropic regularization, and proves gradient consistency under stated conditions. It highlights an extension to the setting where the lower-level feasible set depends on \(x\), for which analogous smoothing-family and gradient-consistency guarantees are not established.
problem_definition_latex:
Construct and analyze a family of continuously differentiable functions \(\{v_\mu:\mu>0\}\) approximating the value function \(v(x)=\min_{y\in Y(x)} g(x,y)\) such that:

1) (Smoothing property) For every \(\bar x\in O\supseteq X\),
\[\lim_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} v_\mu(x)=v(\bar x).\]

2) (Gradient consistency) For every \(\bar x\in O\),
\[\emptyset\neq \limsup_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} \nabla v_\mu(x)\ \subseteq\ \partial v(\bar x).\]

The setting is the bilevel value-function approach with an upper-level-dependent lower-level feasible set \(Y(x)\), and \(g\) Lipschitz continuous but possibly nonsmooth.

Construct and analyze a family of continuously differentiable functions \(\{v_\mu:\mu>0\}\) approximating the value function \(v(x)=\min_{y\in Y(x)} g(x,y)\) such that:

1) (Smoothing property) For every \(\bar x\in O\supseteq X\),
\[\lim_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} v_\mu(x)=v(\bar x).\]

2) (Gradient consistency) For every \(\bar x\in O\),
\[\emptyset\neq \limsup_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} \nabla v_\mu(x)\ \subseteq\ \partial v(\bar x).\]

The setting is the bilevel value-function approach with an upper-level-dependent lower-level feasible set \(Y(x)\), and \(g\) Lipschitz continuous but possibly nonsmooth.
location_in_paper: Section 5 (Concluding Remarks and Future Directions), page 30 (PDF pagination as provided: page 30/32)
open_problem_quote: Another important direction is to extend our smoothing frameworks to settings where the lower-level constraint set is not fixed but instead depends on the upper-level variable x. A preliminary approach based on quadratic regularization combined with penalization has been proposed in [Liu et al., 2023]; however, it remains an open question whether the resulting approximation qualifies as a smoothing family in the sense of Definition 2.5 and whether it satisfies gradient consistency.
subject_classification: bilevel optimization; nonsmooth analysis; variational analysis
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question concerns existence of a smoothing family and gradient-consistency properties in a general parametric-feasible-set setting, not a single concrete inequality that can be falsified by a small finite instance. Automated search could help probe specific constructions, but the space of admissible \(Y(x)\), \(g\), and smoothing schemes is too broad for systematic counterexample mining without additional structure.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: If one fixes a concrete parametric set model \(Y(x)\) and a candidate regularization/penalization template for \(v_\mu\), an automated loop could numerically test approximation error and empirical gradient-consistency surrogates on sampled instances. However, the target properties are asymptotic and set-valued (involving Clarke subgradients), so a reliable automatic fitness/evaluator is nontrivial without narrowing to a specific class where subgradients can be computed or certified.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
